metadata.raw_response_text:
:::writing
Cities say they want people to leave their cars at home. Then they design streets that punish anyone who tries.

Nowhere is that contradiction clearer than on congested urban corridors where buses crawl through the same traffic as private cars. A bus carrying 50 people sits behind a line of vehicles that often carry just one. Everyone waits. Everyone fumes. And transit riders—who are often the people with the fewest transportation choices—pay the price.

Dedicated bus lanes are the simplest, most practical fix.

A single bus lane can move far more people per hour than a lane filled with private cars. When buses are freed from gridlock, they become faster and more reliable. That reliability is crucial: people will choose transit only if they trust it will arrive and get them to work on time. A bus that glides past traffic sends a clear signal that public transportation is not the slow option—it is the smart one.

Critics often worry that removing a general traffic lane will worsen congestion. In practice, cities around the world have shown the opposite can happen. When transit becomes faster and more dependable, some drivers switch to the bus. Streets move more people even if they carry fewer cars.

Dedicated bus lanes also make economic sense. Compared with rail lines or major road expansions, painting a lane and installing clear signage costs relatively little. Yet the payoff can be immediate: shorter travel times, more reliable schedules, and better access to jobs and services for thousands of riders each day.

There is also an equity argument that is hard to ignore. Bus riders are disproportionately students, seniors, and lower‑income workers. Designing streets that prioritize their movement is not a radical idea—it is a fair one.

Of course, dedicated lanes must be enforced. A bus lane that becomes a parking lane or delivery zone during rush hour quickly loses its value. Cameras, clear markings, and consistent rules are essential. Cities should also coordinate loading zones and side‑street access so businesses can continue to operate smoothly.

Urban streets are limited public space. The question is not whether to manage that space, but how. When one lane can move dozens of people at once instead of a handful, the choice should be obvious.

If cities are serious about reducing congestion, improving air quality, and giving residents a real transportation alternative, they should start with the most visible commitment possible: give the bus its own lane—and let it move.
:::
